problem_summary: 2020 MCM/ICM Problem D (operations research / network science): analyze the passing-network and event data of a fictional soccer team ("Huskies") over a 38-game season to (1) characterize team-level cooperation and structure from passing networks, (2) build a general indicator system to evaluate team performance dynamically, (3) advise the coach on improving performance, and (4) generalize the framework to other teamwork settings (business, R&D). This paper attacks all four tasks with two coupled models: a Multi-level Network Analysis Model (macro/micro/middle) for describing play, and a Multi-angle Team Performance Evaluation Model (six attributional factors fused into one index, validated against goal difference).
models_used: Multi-level passing-network analysis: nodes = players sized by total passes, edge widths = pass counts between players, node positions = average (x,y) of a player's passing locations; used to extract the team's de-facto formation (identified as 5-4-1) from match 3

Micro-level tactical pattern recognition: four named short-combination tactics (BPBC back-pass-back-cut, OPSP oblique-pass-straight-penetration, DPOP direct-pass-oblique-penetration, LRT large-range-triangulation) each defined by explicit coordinate inequalities on passer/receiver (x,y) positions, then counted across the season (e.g. OPSP=89, LRT=53, DPOP=40, BPBC=14)

Middle-level tactical-style classification: a 2x2 matrix on (possession rate x ball position per 15-min window) yielding four styles — defensive counterattack, pressure attack, tiki-taka, gegenpressing — tracked per 15-minute segment to produce a per-match style timeline
Pezzali score: (goals/attempts)_team * (attempts/goals)_opponent as a combined offensive/defensive efficiency indicator (borrowed from Cintia et al.)

H indicator: teamwork/flow index combining total passes omega, player pass mean/variance, and zone pass mean/variance over a 10x10 grid, via harmonic-mean-style formula H = 5 / (1/omega + 1/mu_p + 1/sigma_p + 1/mu_z + 1/sigma_z) (Cintia et al.)

Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP): position-specific judgment matrices for forwards/midfielders/defenders over 5 ability dimensions (pass success, ground duel, air duel, smart pass, acceleration), giving distinct weight vectors F/M/D; consistency checked (CI 0.1121, 0.0341, 0.0342, all passed)
Entropy Weight Method (EWM): objective weighting of the six attributional factors (P, H, R1, R2, R3, R4) producing TAI = 0.3575P + 0.0908H + 0.1625R1 + 0.1168R2 + 0.1883R3 + 0.0842R4

Statistical correlation analysis (SPSS) between TAI and goal difference (GD): significance 0.024, claimed significant positive correlation; supported by z-score standardized TAI-vs-GD time series and a Q-Q plot

Confidence-interval-based sensitivity analysis: perturb Pezzali (and H) while holding the other five factors at season averages, reporting 90%/95% CIs for TAI (Pezzali: [2.6381,3.9982]/[2.5079,4.1285]); also outlier removal of game-16 Pezzali (12.5, >5 sigma)
Application/decision layer: formation matchup analysis (4-3-3 vs 4-4-2, five-defender 5-4-1/5-3-2 effectiveness), per-player season ability ranking (best F6/M13/D8), and two recommended 'best squads'
key_techniques: Coordinate-inequality rule encoding: turning vague soccer tactics into machine-countable patterns using only passer/receiver x,y coordinates (e.g. LRT requires x_A>x_B, x_C>x_B, |y_A - y_C|>50), making qualitative 'tactics' fully data-driven
Three-resolution decomposition (macro formation / micro tactic / middle style) so a single network dataset is mined at three altitudes, covering Task 1 completely

Hybrid weighting: AHP (subjective, position-aware) for the player-ability sub-index nested inside EWM (objective, entropy-based) for the top-level six-factor fusion — combining domain priors with data-driven objectivity
Validation against an external ground truth (goal difference) via correlation + Q-Q plot, rather than asserting the index is good by construction
Per-15-minute temporal segmentation to capture in-game tactical switching, and defining 'tactical adaptability' R3 as the number of style changes — turning a timeline into a scalar feature
Outlier hygiene: explicitly detecting and stripping the >5-sigma Pezzali value in game 16 before computing season means/CIs
Narrative anchoring to real match outcomes (the 2-5 loss in game 9 that got the manager fired; the 2-0 win in game 15) to make abstract indices believable to a judge
why_it_won: "From a judge's perspective the paper wins on completeness, traceability, and verification. (1) It answers all four tasks with a single coherent two-model spine and never leaves a sub-question dangling. (2) It converts fuzzy soccer concepts into reproducible, countable definitions — every tactic and style has an explicit math/inequality criterion, so the analysis is auditable rather than hand-waved. (3) It does real validation: instead of just building an index, it correlates TAI against an independent outcome (goal difference) with a reported significance (0.024) and a Q-Q plot, plus a confidence-interval sensitivity analysis — exactly the 'did you test your model' rigor judges reward. (4) It grounds every claim in the actual provided data and ties indices back to concrete season events (game 9 firing, game 15 win), which reads as domain-literate and honest. (5) The weighting is methodologically defensible (AHP with passed consistency tests nested in objective EWM). (6) It delivers concrete, actionable coach advice (play 4-3-3 vs 4-4-2, favor five-defender formations, two named best squads, best player per line) and a thoughtful Task-4 generalization to business/R&D teams. The combination of literature grounding, reproducible definitions, statistical testing, and decision-relevant output is the classic O-paper profile."
section_highlights: "Summary: unusually concrete — names the two models, the six factors, the actual CIs, and the specific coaching recommendations, so a judge knows the whole arc in one page. Literature Review: tightly mapped — each cited work is tied to a specific technique borrowed (Duch->player grouping, Cintia->Pezzali+H, Buldu->network science, Gursakal->small-group motifs), signaling the methods are grounded not invented. Model 1 (3.1): clean macro/micro/middle scaffold with the formation network diagram, the four-tactic schematic + inequality definitions, and the 2x2 style matrix. Model 2 (3.2): explicit six-factor construction, the EWM flow chart, and the final TAI formula with numeric weights. 3.2.3: the validation centerpiece (SPSS correlation 0.024, z-scored TAI-vs-GD line chart, Q-Q plot). 3.3: sensitivity analysis with reported 90/95% CIs and the >5-sigma outlier removal. 3.4: decision layer with formation-matchup bar charts, player ability ranking, and two diagrammed best squads. Section 5: a candid, specific advantages/disadvantages list (admits it can only see ball-touching players, and ignores morale/cohesion). Appendix: AHP judgment matrices with CI values plus runnable MATLAB for AHP and EWM — strong reproducibility signal."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Tiny sample for the statistical claim: ~37-38 matches with a single p=0.024 correlation is weak evidence; no effect size, confidence interval on the correlation, or out-of-sample check, so 'significant positive correlation' is fragile
Formation read from a single game: the 5-4-1 'team formation' is inferred from match 3 only, then treated as representative — no robustness across matches

Tactic definitions are unvalidated heuristics: the coordinate-inequality thresholds (e.g. |y_A - y_C|>50, the half-pitch checks) are asserted without calibration, sensitivity, or false-positive analysis; magic constants appear with no justification

Internal inconsistencies/typos undermine polish: the six factors are described variously (formation appears then disappears; 'player flexibility' vs 'team flexibility'), figures are garbled (Q-Q axis 'DG', radar/bar tables mislabeled), and the EWM weight formula in the flowchart drops parentheses

Sensitivity analysis is narrow: only Pezzali and H are perturbed (the two largest/most-coachable weights), and only one-at-a-time around season means — no joint perturbation of R1-R4, so robustness claims overreach

Self-fulfilling validation risk: TAI is built partly from offensive/efficiency factors (Pezzali uses goals) and then correlated with goal difference, so positive correlation is partly baked in rather than independent confirmation

Acknowledged blind spots: only players who touched the ball are observed (no off-ball movement/defensive shape), and intangible factors (morale, cohesion) are omitted — the authors flag these but they materially limit the tactical conclusions
Best-squad recommendations are not optimized: 'two best squads' are assembled by picking top per-position ability scores, with no lineup optimization, chemistry, or constraint model
reusable_patterns: Multi-altitude decomposition: when given relational/event data, analyze it at macro (aggregate structure), micro (atomic motifs), and middle (temporal regimes) levels so one dataset answers a multi-part prompt
Rule-encode qualitative concepts as coordinate/inequality predicates so fuzzy domain ideas become countable features — directly portable to any 'detect pattern X in trajectory/event data' problem

Nested weighting stack: AHP (domain-prior, group-specific judgment matrices with consistency test) for sub-indicators feeding EWM (objective entropy weights) for the composite — a clean template for 'build one fair score from heterogeneous factors'
Composite-index + external-outcome validation: never present a constructed index alone; correlate it against an independent ground-truth metric and show it with a time series and Q-Q/normality check

Confidence-interval sensitivity analysis: perturb the highest-weight / most-controllable inputs while fixing others at their mean and report 90/95% CIs of the output — cheap, judge-pleasing robustness evidence
Outlier-hygiene note: flag and remove >k-sigma anomalies (and say so explicitly) before computing means/CIs to pre-empt referee skepticism
Anchor abstract metrics to concrete real events in the data (specific games/outcomes) to make the model legible and persuasive

Decision layer that ends in named, concrete recommendations (specific formations, specific players, two ready lineups) plus an honest advantages/disadvantages section and a Task-4 generalization to analogous domains
Reproducibility kit: include judgment matrices, CI values, and runnable code (AHP + EWM MATLAB) in an appendix
